title: Chamomile melt-and-pour soap
subtitle: A gentle, golden-tinted bar made with chamomile tea infusion and calendula-oil enriched base
excerpt: Chamomile tea used in place of the water phase gives this melt-and-pour bar a pale golden colour and a very mild, hay-like fragrance. Suitable for sensitive and reactive skin types. The bar is ready to use within two hours of pouring.
sourceNotes: Chamomile tea infusion technique in melt-and-pour soap from Bramble Berry beginner guide (brambleberry.com, May 2026). Chamomile skin properties from EMA HMPC monograph on Matricaria recutita (2015).

recipe.yieldDescription: Approx 450 g soap (~3 to 4 bars, depending on mould size)
recipe.batchNotes: Scale proportionally. Brew a proportionally larger batch of chamomile tea.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Brew and cool the chamomile tea the day before. The bar can be used immediately after unmoulding.
recipe.temperatureNote: Add the chamomile tea infusion when the melted base has cooled to around 65 degrees Celsius.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: 18 months wrapped in a cool, dry place. Melt-and-pour bars made with botanical infusions have a shorter shelf life than plain glycerin soap because of the residual plant compounds. Wrap in cling film or wax paper. Discard if the bar develops a sour smell or shows visible mould.

glossaryTerms.definition: A pre-saponified glycerin soap base that requires only melting, scenting, and pouring. No lye handling is involved. Clear base shows the golden chamomile colour most effectively.

content.text: tints the bar a warm gold and carries a very faint floral-hay note that does not clash with other scents. Use a clear glycerin base to show the colour; a white opaque base will mask it. Brew a strong tea (4 bags or 4 teaspoons of loose flowers to 120 ml of water) and cool it completely before adding it to the melted base. Add more than 30 ml of liquid to a 450 g base and the bar may not set firmly; 20 to 30 ml is the right range.

items.prepNote: Brewed as a strong tea in 120 ml boiling water; strained and cooled
Optional; adds a cleaner chamomile note; add at 65 degrees Celsius

content.text: Brew the chamomile tea: steep the dried flowers in 120 ml of just-boiled water for 10 minutes. Strain through a fine sieve or muslin cloth. Allow the tea to cool to room temperature. You will use 20 to 30 ml of the cooled tea.

Chop the soap base into cubes and melt in a pouring pitcher over a simmering-water bath or in a microwave in 30-second bursts. Stir between each burst. When the base reaches 70 degrees Celsius and is fully liquid, remove from heat and allow to cool to 65 degrees Celsius. Stir in 25 ml of the chamomile tea, the roman chamomile essential oil, and lavender essential oil if using. Mix gently. Pour into the mould. Spritz the surface with isopropyl alcohol to clear any bubbles. Leave undisturbed for 2 hours before unmoulding.

Wrap each bar in cling film immediately after unmoulding. Unwrapped melt-and-pour soap absorbs atmospheric moisture and becomes sticky. Bars made with botanical tea infusions keep for 18 months in a cool, dry place. The chamomile colour may fade to pale gold or beige over time; this does not affect performance. Discard any bar with a sour smell or visible surface growth.
